title: Variance and Standard Deviation
concept: Variance and standard deviation measure the spread of a dataset, showing how far data points typically sit from the mean.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Which metric should you use to find the center of a dataset with extreme outliers, like household wealth?
exercises.explain: The median is robust against extreme outliers, whereas the mean gets dragged toward them.

cards.title: Two cities, same average temp
cards.body: San Francisco and Denver both have an average annual temperature of about 55°F. But SF stays mild year-round, while Denver has freezing winters and hot summers. Average alone doesn't tell the whole story.

cards.title: Measuring the spread
cards.body: To capture consistency, we use variance (the average squared distance from the mean) and standard deviation (the square root of variance). They measure how spread out the data is.

cards.title: The target practice
cards.body: Think of an archer. If all arrows hit close to each other (even if not in the bullseye), their spread is low (low standard deviation). If arrows are scattered all over the target, their spread is high.

cards.title: Why ML cares
cards.body: ML models use standard deviation to normalize data. If one feature (like house size in square feet) has a massive spread and another (like number of bedrooms) has a tiny spread, the model gets confused. We scale them to have similar spreads.

cards.title: Variance isn't in normal units
cards.body: Because variance squares the distances, its units are squared (like 'square degrees' or 'square dollars'). Standard deviation fixes this by taking the square root, returning the spread to the original unit (degrees or dollars).

left.text:
Temps cluster close to 55°F
Consistent, predictable weather

right.text:
Temps spread far from 55°F
Wide swings, unpredictable day

exercises.prompt: If a dataset has a standard deviation of zero, what does this tell us about the data points?

options.text: The mean of the dataset is zero

exercises.explain: Standard deviation measures spread. If the spread is zero, there is no deviation at all — every single number in the dataset is exactly the same.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Standard deviation is calculated in the same units as the original data (e.g., dollars or meters).
exercises.explain: True! While variance uses squared units, taking the square root to get standard deviation brings the measurement back to the original unit.

exercises.sentence: To calculate standard deviation, you must first calculate the ___.
exercises.explain: Standard deviation is the square root of the variance, so you must find the variance first.

exercises.prompt: Why do we typically prefer standard deviation over variance when explaining data spread to humans?

options.text: Standard deviation is in the original unit of the data, making it easy to interpret.
Standard deviation is always a smaller number.

exercises.explain: Variance is in squared units (e.g. 'squared dollars'), which is hard to conceptualize. Standard deviation converts it back to normal units (e.g. 'dollars').

exercises.prompt: You are training an ML model to detect fraud. Normal users make purchases with a low standard deviation in transaction sizes (most are $10-$20). A suspect account suddenly has a standard deviation of $500. What does this mean?

options.text: The suspect's spending amounts are highly scattered, varying wildly from their average.
The suspect's average transaction size is exactly $500.
The suspect's transactions are extremely consistent.
The suspect has made zero transactions.

exercises.explain: Exactly! A high standard deviation means the transaction amounts fluctuate wildly, which is a classic flag for anomalous behavior. Fantastic job mastering spread!
